text: Tell me a little about what brings you in today and what's been going on with your drinking.
speaker: therapist

text: Well, my wife has been on my case about it. She thinks I drink too much, but honestly I don't think it's that big of a deal. I have a couple of beers after work to unwind, maybe more on weekends.
speaker: client
dialogue_context: Therapist: Tell me a little about what brings you in today and what's been going on with your drinking.

text: So on one hand, you don't see your drinking as a major problem, but on the other hand, it's important enough to your wife that it's causing some tension between you two.
speaker: therapist
dialogue_context: Client: Well, my wife has been on my case about it. She thinks I drink too much, but honestly I don't think it's that big of a deal. I have a couple of beers after work to unwind, maybe more on weekends.

text: Yeah, exactly. I mean, I love my wife and I don't want this to come between us. But I also feel like I should be able to relax the way I want after a long day.
speaker: client
dialogue_context: Therapist: So on one hand, you don't see your drinking as a major problem, but on the other hand, it's important enough to your wife that it's causing some tension between you two.

text: Your relationship with your wife is really important to you. What would it mean for your marriage if things continued the way they are right now?
speaker: therapist
dialogue_context: Client: Yeah, exactly. I mean, I love my wife and I don't want this to come between us. But I also feel like I should be able to relax the way I want after a long day.

text: I guess I'm a little worried about that, to be honest. We've been arguing more lately, and not just about the drinking. I know I'm more irritable when I've had a few drinks. I don't want to lose her.
speaker: client
dialogue_context: Therapist: Your relationship with your wife is really important to you. What would it mean for your marriage if things continued the way they are right now?

text: It takes a lot of courage to be honest about that. You're noticing a connection between your drinking and the arguments, and you clearly care deeply about your marriage.
speaker: therapist
dialogue_context: Client: I guess I'm a little worried about that, to be honest. We've been arguing more lately, and not just about the drinking. I know I'm more irritable when I've had a few drinks. I don't want to lose her.

text: But I don't know if I can just stop. All my friends drink. It's what we do when we get together. I'd feel weird being the only one not drinking.
speaker: client
dialogue_context: Therapist: It takes a lot of courage to be honest about that. You're noticing a connection between your drinking and the arguments, and you clearly care deeply about your marriage.

text: A lot of people feel that way, and it makes sense that the social aspect is important to you. If you were to make a change, it wouldn't necessarily have to be all or nothing. What are some small steps you could imagine taking?
speaker: therapist
dialogue_context: Client: But I don't know if I can just stop. All my friends drink. It's what we do when we get together. I'd feel weird being the only one not drinking.

text: I suppose I could try cutting back on the weekday drinking. Maybe just save it for weekends. And I could try to set a limit when I'm out with friends, like two or three drinks max. I think I could do that.
speaker: client
dialogue_context: Therapist: A lot of people feel that way, and it makes sense that the social aspect is important to you. If you were to make a change, it wouldn't necessarily have to be all or nothing. What are some small steps you could imagine taking?
